section.title: The Lord's Choice of Zion
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, thirteenth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. The longest and most detailed of the Ascents, it recalls David's costly zeal to find a dwelling for the LORD and rests the whole psalm on the LORD's own sworn covenant with David and His chosen dwelling in Zion.
section.thesis: David's costly vow to find a dwelling for the LORD is answered by the LORD's own greater and unbreakable oath, that one of David's own descendants would sit forever on his throne and that the LORD Himself would dwell in Zion forever, a covenant fulfilled in Christ, the horn raised up for David and the King whose throne endures forever.
section.frameNote: Four stanzas: David's oath and costly zeal to find a dwelling for the LORD, verses 1 to 5, the ark's journey and the people's prayer, verses 6 to 10, the LORD's sworn oath to David's house, verses 11 to 12, and the LORD's chosen dwelling in Zion with its promised blessings, verses 13 to 18.

verses.text: O LORD, remember on behalf of David all the hardships he endured,
how he swore an oath to the LORD, and vowed to the Mighty One of Jacob:
"I will not enter my house or get into my bed,
I will not give sleep to my eyes or slumber to my eyelids,
until I find a place for the LORD, a dwelling for the Mighty One of Jacob."

units.label: Let us go to His dwelling place
units.note: the ark's journey and the people's prayer

verses.text: We heard that the ark was in Ephrathah; we found it in the fields of Jaar.
Let us go to His dwelling place; let us worship at His footstool.
Arise, O LORD, to Your resting place, You and the ark of Your strength.
May Your priests be clothed with righteousness, and Your saints shout for joy.
For the sake of Your servant David, do not reject Your {{mashiach|anointed one}}.

units.label: The LORD swore an oath to David

verses.text: The LORD swore an oath to David, a promise He will not revoke: "One of your descendants I will place on your throne.
If your sons keep My covenant and the testimony I will teach them, then their sons will also sit on your throne forever and ever."

units.label: For the LORD has chosen Zion

verses.text: For the LORD has chosen Zion; He has desired it for His home:
"This is My resting place forever and ever; here I will dwell, for I have desired this home.
I will bless her with abundant provisions; I will satisfy her poor with bread.
I will clothe her priests with salvation, and her saints will sing out in joy.
There I will make a {{qeren|horn}} grow for David; I have prepared a lamp for My {{mashiach|anointed one}}.
I will clothe his enemies with shame, but the crown upon him will gleam."

qeren.gloss: A horn, symbol of strength, dignity, and vigor, as on a bull or ram; the LORD promises to make a horn grow for David, a coming king of unfailing strength, verse 17.

mashiach.gloss: Anointed one; a king or priest set apart by anointing oil for the LORD's purposes. Here it names David's royal line, verses 10 and 17, the title from which Messiah and Christ come.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents recalls David's costly, sleepless zeal to find a dwelling for the LORD, the ark's journey from Kiriath-jearim to Jerusalem, and rests everything on the LORD's own sworn covenant, that David's line would hold the throne forever and that He Himself had chosen Zion as His resting place.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes a specific, historic covenant with David and choice of Zion, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern zealous devotion to God's presence and confident trust in His own sworn, unbreakable promises, which run ahead of and beyond any human vow.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm turns on mashiach, anointed one, naming David's royal line, and qeren, horn, an image of vigorous strength promised to sprout for David, both titles that flower into the New Testament's Messiah and Christ.
basic.typology: The horn made to grow for David and the lamp prepared for the LORD's anointed anticipate Christ, the horn of salvation raised up in David's house, and the sworn, unbreakable oath that one of David's descendants would reign forever anticipates the eternal throne of Christ, great David's greater Son.
basic.christ: Zechariah's prophecy over his son John blesses God for raising up 'a horn of salvation for us in the house of His servant David,' Luke 1:69, directly quoting this psalm's language, and Peter preaches at Pentecost that God swore an oath to David that one of his descendants would sit on his throne, fulfilled when God raised Jesus and seated Him at His right hand, Acts 2:30.
basic.god: The LORD answers David's costly zeal with His own far greater, unbreakable oath, choosing Zion as His dwelling place forever, promising abundant provision and joy for His people, and swearing that David's throne would be occupied by a descendant whose reign endures forever and ever.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 1:69 quotes this psalm's promise of a horn raised up in David's house, fulfilled in Christ. Acts 2:30 cites God's oath to David that a descendant would sit on his throne, fulfilled in Jesus's resurrection and exaltation. Hebrews 12:22 shows believers coming to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the fulfillment of the LORD's chosen dwelling.
basic.oneStory: David's costly zeal to build a house for the LORD is answered by the LORD's greater covenant oath, fulfilled in Christ, the horn of David raised up and the King whose throne, unlike every earthly reign, will never be shaken or pass away.

context.literary: The longest of the Songs of Ascents, structured in four clear stanzas moving from David's personal vow, to the ark's journey and communal prayer, to the LORD's own covenant oath, to His chosen dwelling and its promised blessings.
context.historical: Recalls the historical episode of David bringing the ark from Kiriath-jearim, 'the fields of Jaar,' to Jerusalem, 2 Samuel 6, and rests on the covenant God made with David through Nathan the prophet, 2 Samuel 7:12-16.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 131's settled, humble trust with the grand, covenantal ground for that trust, the LORD's own sworn oath to David and Zion, and anticipates the New Testament's announcement that this covenant finds its heir in Jesus, the Son of David.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To recall David's costly devotion to secure a dwelling for the LORD, and to ground Israel's ongoing hope not in David's vow but in the LORD's own far greater, unbreakable oath concerning David's throne and His chosen dwelling in Zion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical covenant made with David and the LORD's choice of Zion as His resting place. It prescribes as an abiding pattern zealous devotion to seeking God's presence and unwavering confidence in God's own sworn promises, which do not depend on human faithfulness alone.

debates.question: Is the conditional clause of verse 12, 'if your sons keep My covenant,' a threat to the unconditional promise of verse 11, or a distinct layer within it?

views.label: A conditional layer governing ordinary succession
views.case: Verse 12 explicitly conditions the throne's occupation by each successive son on covenant faithfulness, explaining why many of David's descendants were removed or judged.

views.label: An unconditional promise ultimately secured in one final descendant
views.case: Verse 11's 'promise He will not revoke' points to an unconditional core guarantee that the line would not fail entirely, ultimately secured not in any sinful son's obedience but in the perfect obedience of Christ, the final Son of David.

debates.bearing: Together the verses explain both the many failures and removals within David's line and the certainty of its ultimate fulfillment in Christ, whose perfect obedience secures forever what no other son of David could.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm's climax turns on qeren, horn, an image of vigorous strength promised to sprout for David, verse 17, and mashiach, anointed one, verses 10 and 17, the royal title that flowers across Scripture into Messiah and, in Greek, Christ.

typology.type: The horn made to grow for David and the lamp prepared for the LORD's anointed, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the horn of salvation raised up in the house of God's servant David, Luke 1:69.

typology.type: The LORD's sworn, unbreakable oath that a descendant of David would reign forever, verses 11 to 12
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, raised and seated on David's throne, whose kingdom will never end, Acts 2:30, Luke 1:32-33.

study.christ: Zechariah, filled with the Holy Spirit, blesses God for having 'raised up a horn of salvation for us in the house of His servant David,' Luke 1:69, quoting this psalm's very language and applying it to the coming of Jesus. At Pentecost, Peter preaches that God had sworn an oath to David that one of his descendants would sit on his throne, and declares this fulfilled when God raised Jesus from the dead and exalted Him, Acts 2:30. Christ is the horn that sprouted for David and the anointed one whose crown, unlike David's fallen throne, will never fade.
study.god: The LORD answers David's costly, sleepless zeal with His own far greater and unbreakable oath, choosing Zion as His dwelling place forever, promising abundant provision, salvation, and joy for His people, and swearing that David's throne would finally and forever be filled by a descendant whose reign never ends.

crossReferences.note: Zechariah's prophecy quotes this psalm's promise of a horn raised up in David's house, fulfilled in Christ.
Peter cites God's oath to David that a descendant would sit on his throne, fulfilled in Jesus's resurrection and exaltation.
Believers have come to Mount Zion, the city of the living God, the fulfillment of the LORD's chosen and everlasting dwelling.

study.oneStory: David's costly zeal to build a dwelling for the LORD is answered by the LORD's own greater covenant oath, fulfilled in Christ, the horn of David raised up and the King whose throne, unlike every earthly reign before Him, will never be shaken or pass away.

sermon.title: A Throne, a Family, a Celebration
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 132 to 134 together, showing how this psalm's covenant oath to David and choice of Zion grounds the pilgrim's confidence and joy in the LORD's enduring promises.

quiz.q: What did David vow, according to verses 3 to 5?

options.text: That he would not rest until he found a dwelling place for the LORD.
options.note: This is the exact, costly vow the psalm attributes to David.

options.text: That he would conquer every neighboring nation first.
options.note: The vow concerns finding a dwelling for the LORD, not military conquest.

options.text: That he would build himself a grander palace than any king before him.
options.note: The vow is about a dwelling for the LORD, not David's own personal palace.

options.text: That he would delay any action until his old age.
options.note: The vow describes urgent, sleepless zeal to act soon, not a delay until old age.

quiz.q: According to verse 6, where had the ark been found?

options.text: In Ephrathah, in the fields of Jaar.
options.note: This is the exact location named in the psalm, referring to Kiriath-jearim.

options.text: In the wilderness of Sinai.
options.note: The location named is Ephrathah and the fields of Jaar, not the wilderness of Sinai.

options.text: In the land of the Philistines permanently.
options.note: The ark had earlier been among the Philistines but is here recalled as found in the fields of Jaar, not remaining there.

options.text: In Egypt, before the exodus.
options.note: This location is unrelated to the ark's history recalled in this psalm.

quiz.q: What did the LORD swear to David in verse 11?

options.text: That one of David's descendants would be placed on his throne.
options.note: This is the exact, unbreakable oath the psalm attributes to the LORD.

options.text: That David's line would end within his own lifetime.
options.note: The oath promises the continuation of David's line on the throne, not its ending.

options.text: That David himself would live forever on earth.
options.note: The oath concerns David's descendants occupying the throne, not David's own immortality.

options.text: That the throne would pass to a different family entirely.
options.note: The oath specifically names one of David's own descendants, not another family line.

quiz.q: What has the LORD chosen, according to verse 13?

options.text: Zion, as His desired dwelling place.
options.note: This is the psalm's explicit declaration of the LORD's chosen home.

options.text: Babylon, as a temporary resting place.
options.note: The psalm names Zion, not Babylon, as the LORD's chosen dwelling.

options.text: A place yet to be revealed in the future.
options.note: The psalm names Zion specifically and directly, not an undisclosed future location.

options.text: Every nation equally, without any particular choice.
options.note: The psalm specifically singles out Zion as chosen, not an equal distribution among all nations.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply this psalm's promise of a horn raised up for David?

options.text: Zechariah praises God for raising up a horn of salvation in the house of His servant David, fulfilled in Christ.
options.note: Luke 1:69 directly quotes this psalm's language and applies it to Jesus.

options.text: It applies the horn only to Solomon's temple building.
options.note: Luke 1:69 applies the horn of salvation to the coming of Christ, not merely to temple construction.

options.text: It rejects any connection between this psalm and the coming Messiah.
options.note: The New Testament directly and explicitly draws this connection rather than rejecting it.

options.text: It applies the horn to John the Baptist as the final fulfillment.
options.note: Zechariah's prophecy about the horn points to Jesus, the Son of David, not to John himself as the final fulfillment.

questions: David would not rest until he found a dwelling for the LORD. What comparable, costly zeal for God's presence marks your own life, or is currently missing from it?
The people prayed, 'Let us go to His dwelling place, let us worship at His footstool.' What would it look like for you to approach corporate worship this week with that same eager expectation?
The LORD's oath to David did not depend on David's own faithfulness but on God's unbreakable promise. Where do you need to rest in God's sure promises rather than your own performance?

The LORD chose Zion as His dwelling and promised to bless her poor, clothe her priests with salvation, and cause her saints to sing for joy. How does knowing God delights to dwell among His people shape your sense of belonging in the church?
Christ is the horn raised up for David and the anointed one whose crown will never fade. How does the certainty of Christ's eternal reign give you confidence in the face of today's uncertainties?

translationNotes.note: Ephrathah is associated with the region of Bethlehem, and the fields of Jaar refer to Kiriath-jearim, where the ark rested for about twenty years before David brought it to Jerusalem, 1 Samuel 7:1-2.
One of your descendants is literally 'of the fruit of your body,' Hebrew idiom for a direct physical heir, cited in this form in Acts 2:30 regarding Christ's resurrection.
Horn is a common Hebrew image for strength and honor, often pictured as sprouting or growing, as an animal's horn does, a fitting image for a rising king of unfailing strength.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 132 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (18) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 132:1-18. Footnote markers [a][b][c] present on biblehub for verses 5, 6, and 11 were stripped per instructions; no em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'A Throne, a Family, a Celebration' (covering Psalms 132-134) at truthforlife.org.
